questions.prompt: What is the correct past subjunctive of 'tener' (yo form)?
questions.explanation: Tener → preterite ellos: tuvieron → remove -ron → tuvie- → add -ra → tuviera.

options: Mi mamá quería que yo estudié más.
Mi mamá quería que yo estudiara más.
Mi mamá quería que yo estudiaré más.
Mi mamá quería que yo estudiaba más.
questions.explanation: 'Quería que' is a past-tense trigger requiring the past subjunctive 'estudiara'.

questions.prompt: Which conjunction ALWAYS requires the past subjunctive?
questions.explanation: 'Como si' (as if) always triggers the past subjunctive, even when the main verb is in the present tense.

questions.prompt: What does 'Ojalá tuvieras más paciencia' mean?
options: Hopefully you will have more patience.
I wish you had more patience.
You had a lot of patience.
I hope you have more patience.
questions.explanation: 'Ojalá + past subjunctive' expresses a present wish that is unfulfilled — implying the person currently doesn't have enough patience.

questions.prompt: What is the past subjunctive stem of 'decir'?
questions.explanation: Decir → preterite ellos: dijeron → remove -ron → dije- → dijera, dijeras, etc.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Era importante que todos ___ a tiempo.' (llegar)
questions.explanation: 'Era importante que' is a past-tense trigger. Past subjunctive of llegar: llegaran.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'quisiera' as a polite request?
options: All of the above are correct polite uses.
questions.explanation: 'Quisiera' (past subjunctive of querer) functions as an extremely polite 'I would like' in all three examples, softening requests and wishes.

questions.prompt: Which form is preferred in spoken Mexican Spanish?
options: Neither — past subjunctive is avoided in speech
questions.explanation: In Mexican Spanish, the -ra form is strongly preferred in everyday speech. The -se form exists and is grammatically correct but is mainly found in literary or very formal writing.
